entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content: Remember to "persist" the messages so you don't end up losing events in case the consumer becomes unavailable.

entry.title: May Developer Challenge - Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload
entry.content:
Welcome to Week 3! We're halfway through the challenge and the pipeline is taking shape. You can produce events, get them to a broker, and consume them from code/an integration platform. Now we get to the part that ties this challenge to AI: vectorization.

Links:

  • May's developer challenge blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/may-2026-developer-challenge-from-events-to-intelligence-building-an-event/ba-p/14386772
  • Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
  • Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
  • Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload
  • Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG

This week, we take the data field from the Business Partner event payload and convert it into a vector embedding — a numerical representation of the content that captures its semantic meaning. This is the step that will later allow us to do similarity searches and power a RAG application.

A quick primer on embeddings

An embedding is a list of floating-point numbers — a vector — that represents the meaning of a piece of text in a high-dimensional space. Text with similar meaning ends up close together in that space. This is what makes semantic search possible: instead of matching exact keywords, you match meaning.

To generate embeddings, you need an embedding model. You pass in a piece of text, and it returns a vector. For our purposes, we'll be embedding the content of the data field of our Business Partner events — typically after converting the JSON object to a string or extracting the most relevant fields.

Embedding models in SAP AI Core

For example, from this event payload:

{
  "BusinessPartner": "1003783",
  "BusinessPartnerUUID": "456872b9-b9a2-4b93-894d-dff37abd3070",
  "BusinessPartnerFullName": "Daniela-Anita Macedo",
  "BusinessPartnerCategory": "1",
  "BusinessPartnerGrouping": "BP02",
  "FirstName": "Daniela-Anita",
  "LastName": "Macedo",
  "IsNaturalPerson": "X",
  "CreationDate": "/Date(1518393600000)/",
  "CreatedByUser": "CC0000000002",
  "BusinessPartnerAddress": {
    "Country": "PT",
    "Region": "",
    "CityName": "Quarteira",
    "PostalCode": "1385-831",
    "StreetName": "Travessa de Sousa, 6",
    "HouseNumber": "681",
    "AddressTimeZone": "WEST"
  }
}

You might produce a string like:

BusinessPartner: 1003783. Name: Daniela-Anita Macedo. Category: 1. CityName: Quarteira.

And that string is what you send to the embedding model.

Your task this week

👉 Extend your consumer from Week 2 so that, after receiving a Business Partner event, it generates a vector embedding of the event's data field.

Steps:

  1. Receive the event (as you did in Week 2)
  2. Extract the data field and prepare it as a string
  3. Send that string to an embedding model and get back a vector
  4. Log the vector (or a truncated version of it) to confirm it's working

Embedding model options:

  • SAP options: SAP AI Core (via the Generative AI Hub) — models like text-embedding-3-small_autogenerated or similar are available depending on your setup
  • Open-source / cloud options: OpenAI Embeddings API, HuggingFace Sentence Transformers (fully local, no API key needed), Ollama with a local embedding model

If you want to run everything locally without any API keys, HuggingFace Sentence Transformers is an excellent option. A model like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is small, fast, and produces 384-dimensional embeddings that are more than sufficient for this challenge.

Share your work

Add a comment in this discussion with:

  1. A snippet of code/screenshot showing how you prepared the event data and called your embedding model
  2. A truncated example of the vector embedding you received back (e.g., the first 10 dimensions)
  3. Which embedding model/service you used and why

SAP solution note — I will share how I solved this using SAP AI Core (Generative AI Hub) in the comments below

Some food for thought:

  1. Does the order or structure of the fields you include in your text affect the quality of the embeddings?
  2. If a Business Partner record has missing fields, how would you handle that before sending it to the embedding model?
  3. What is the trade-off between using a cloud-hosted embedding model vs. a locally hosted one?

entry.title: May Developer Challenge - Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG
entry.content:
Welcome to the final week of the May Developer Challenge! 🎉

Links:

  • May's developer challenge blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/may-2026-developer-challenge-from-events-to-intelligence-building-an-event/ba-p/14386772
  • Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
  • Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
  • Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload
  • Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG

This is where everything comes together. Events are flowing through a broker, being consumed from your code/by an integration platform, and being vectorized. Now we store those vectors in a vector store and wire up a simple RAG query to prove the pipeline is working end to end.

What is RAG?

Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a pattern where, instead of asking a large language model (LLM) to answer a question from memory alone, you first "retrieve" relevant context from a knowledge base and then "augment" the LLM's prompt with that context. The LLM generates a response "grounded" in your data rather than generating its response based on the data it's been trained with.

In our case, the "knowledge base" is the vector store we've been building — populated with embeddings of Business Partner events. A user could ask something like "Which business partners are in Germany?" and the RAG pipeline would:

  1. Embed the question using the same model you used in Week 3
  2. Search the vector store for the most similar Business Partner vectors
  3. Include those results in the LLM prompt
  4. Return a grounded, contextual answer

Your task this week

👉 Store the vectors generated last week into a vector store, then write a simple RAG query that retrieves relevant Business Partners based on a natural language question.

Steps:

  1. After generating the embedding in Week 3, store the vector along with the original event metadata (the data field) in a vector store
  2. Implement a query function: take a natural language question, embed it, search the vector store for the top-N most similar vectors, and return the associated Business Partner data
  3. (Optional but encouraged) Pass those results into an LLM to generate a natural language answer

Vector store options:

  • SAP options: SAP HANA Cloud - vector engine — SAP HANA Cloud has had native vector capabilities for some time now.
  • Open-source options: pgvector (Postgres extension)

Share your work

Reply to this post with:

  1. A screenshot or code snippet showing Business Partner vectors being stored in your vector store
  2. A sample query and the results returned (which Business Partners were retrieved?)
  3. (Optional) The LLM-generated answer if you wired up the full RAG response

Things to think about:

  1. What metadata did you store alongside the vector? Why does that matter for the quality of the RAG responses?
  2. How would you keep your vector store up to date as Business Partners change in the source system? (Hint: think about the event-driven pipeline you just built.)
  3. What's the difference between semantic similarity search and full-text search? In which scenarios would you prefer one over the other?

Mastering Topic Taxonomies for Scalable Event-Driven Architectures
entry.content:
A single poorly named topic can break downstream consumers, trigger routing surprises, and make growth painful. These mistakes are common, and they are fixable once a clear taxonomy is in place. This session gives SAP developers, integration architects, and event-driven practitioners a practical, opinionated path to get topic structure right from day one.

Join Solace Developer Advocate Hari Rangarajan and SAP Developer Advocate Antonio Maradiaga for a hands-on webinar focused on topic taxonomy best practices in SAP Advanced Event Mesh. A topic is the structured address that routes an event from producer to consumer. A topic taxonomy is the deliberate, hierarchical naming convention that makes those addresses predictable, governable, and reusable across teams and systems.

We start with a concise primer that clarifies what topics are in SAP Advanced Event Mesh, why naming decisions shape routing and subscriptions, and how a single off-pattern name can ripple into broken consumers downstream. From there, you will follow a live Event Portal walkthrough inside an application domain that contrasts solid patterns with common anti-patterns. Flat, opaque, or overly generic topic strings lead to real problems: failed event discovery, fan-out explosions, and governance headaches that compound as your system scales. You will see the impact in real time, and you can ask questions as we iterate on designs.

Next, we look at SAP S/4HANA. Its native business events ship with a topic structure that aligns with proven taxonomy principles, which helps teams extend consistently and integrate without reinventing conventions.

We close with queue naming conventions: clear rules for labeling durable message queues so teams can read, operate, and evolve them confidently across environments. You will see the decision trade-offs and a concrete example in the Event Portal.

What you will learn:

  • How to design a scalable, hierarchical topic taxonomy for SAP Advanced Event Mesh
  • The most common topic anti-patterns, why they happen, and how to avoid them
  • How SAP S/4HANA native events align with best-practice topic structures
  • Queue naming conventions: guidelines, decision trade-offs, and a live Event Portal walkthrough
  • How to use the Event Portal application domain view to govern and validate your event landscape

Save your seat and bring your use cases for the live Q&A.
